        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Results.We investigated the impact of PDR geometry and agreed on the comparison of results from spherical and plane-parallel PDR models. We identified a number of key processes governing the chemical network which have been treated differently in the various codes such as the effect of PAHs on the electron density or the temperature dependence of the dissociation of CO by cosmic ray induced secondary photons, and defined a proper common treatment. We established a comprehensive set of reference models for ongoing and future PDR model bench-marking and were able to increase the agreement in model predictions for all benchmark models significantly. Nevertheless, the remaining spread in the computed observables such as the atomic fine-structure line intensities serves as a warning that there is still a considerable uncertainty when interpreting astronomical data with our models.\n\nAims.We present a comparison between independent computer codes, modeling the physics and chemistry of interstellar photon dominated regions (PDRs). Our goal was to understand the mutual differences in the PDR codes and their effects on the physical and chemical structure of the model clouds, and to converge the output of different codes to a common solution. Methods. A number of benchmark models have been created, covering low and high gas densities cm-3 and far ultraviolet intensities \u03c7 = 10, 105 in units of the Draine field (FUV: 6 < < 13.6 eV). The benchmark models were computed in two ways: one set assuming constant temperatures, thus testing the consistency of the chemical network and photo-processes, and a second set determining the temperature self consistently by solving the thermal balance, thus testing the modeling of the heating and cooling mechanisms accounting for the detailed energy balance throughout the clouds.\n\nResults.We investigated the impact of PDR geometry and agreed on the comparison of results from spherical and plane-parallel PDR models. We identified a number of key processes governing the chemical network which have been treated differently in the various codes such as the effect of PAHs on the electron density or the temperature dependence of the dissociation of CO by cosmic ray induced secondary photons, and defined a proper common treatment. We established a comprehensive set of reference models for ongoing and future PDR model bench-marking and were able to increase the agreement in model predictions for all benchmark models significantly. Nevertheless, the remaining spread in the computed observables such as the atomic fine-structure line intensities serves as a warning that there is still a considerable uncertainty when interpreting astronomical data with our models.\n\nM.         "text": "Yet others aim at efficient and ... their physical and chemical structure, the choice of free parameters, and other details. Consequently it is not always straightforward to directly compare the results from different PDR codes. Taking into account that there are multiple ways of implementing physical effects in numerical codes, it is obvious that the model output of different PDR codes can differ from each other. More details on the PDR Comparison Study. We use an updated version of the spherical PDR code described in detail by R\u00f6llig et al....\n\nYet others aim at efficient and rapid calculation of large model grids for comparison with observational data, which comes at the cost of pragmatic approximations using effective rates rather than detailed treatment. As a result, the different models have focused on the detailed simulation of particular processes determining the structure in the main regions of interest while using only rough approximations for other processes. The model setups vary strongly among different model codes. This includes the assumed model geometry, their physical and chemical structure, the choice of free parameters, and other details. Consequently it is not always straightforward to directly compare the results from different PDR codes. Taking into account that there are multiple ways of implementing physical effects in numerical codes, it is obvious that the model output of different PDR codes can differ from each other. More details on the PDR Comparison Study. We use an updated version of the spherical\n\nThe main work goes into the improved modelling of the microphysical and chemical processes in the KOSMA-tau PDR model. This means in particular accounting for non-stationary effects: to include the full time-dependent chemistry to the model. This will allow to model the impact of : ... Markus R\u00f6llig and Yoko Okada are Co-Investigators of the ERS project \"Radiative Feedback from Massive Stars as Traced by Multiband Imaging and Spectroscopic Mosaics\". As preparation for the launch of JWST, we are updating KOSMA-tau to solve the full H2 problem (full ro-vib structure). In addition we need to account for non-stationary PAH heating. This is work in progress. KOSMA-tau results will be made available on the PDR model database ISMDB of the Meudon group in order to allow a comparison between both models. Schneider et al. (1993), we now account for all important processes. We also developed an improved description of the chemistry induced desorption (e.g. Cazaux et al. 2016). A respective publication is in preparation. improvement of numerical stability and convergence We included an additional set of numerical solvers to the chemical solution module of the model: DGESV, DGELSD (least squares), DGESVX are now available to solve the chemical rate equation system.\n\nextending the dust treatment to cover arbitrary dust distributions We implemented an improved dust treatment including the continuum radiative transfer as well as the dust temperature computation for an arbitrary dust content of the model cloud. Using the description by Weingartner and Draine (2001) we can now account for different compositions and dust size distributions of large grains, very small grains and PAHs. The full description of this update can be found in R\u00f6llig et al. (2013). In a recent update we extended this description to also account for low metallicity environments, such as the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds. This was used in a recent work by Okada et al.         "text": "These considerations mean that solving for the 3D structure of a PDR can be computationally expensive. For this reason, most numerical codes used to model PDRs are confined to a 1D geometry. Most of these models consider a slab of material that is irradiated from one direction by an FUV field. The slab is often considered to be semi-infinite, so that photons can only escape in a direction opposite to that of the incoming radiation. Alternatively, some models (e.g. R\u00f6llig et al., 2006) consider a spherical geometry and an isotropic FUV field. In either case, these models have the advantage that a range of different UV fields and density structures can be modelled relatively quickly. Most 1D codes also assume that the medium is smooth or uniform, though the clumpy, fractal, nature of the ISM can be approximated by considering ensembles of 1D calculations.\n\nNevertheless, in recent years there have been a number of developments in the three-dimensional modelling of PDRs. Glover and Mac Low (2007a,b) and Glover et al. (2010) carried out hydrodynamical simulations of turbulent gas in the PDR regime, using a six-ray approximation to model the attenuation of the external radiation field (see also Nelson and Langer, 1997). In this approximation, the absorption of UV radiation by H2 and CO molecules and by dust is followed along a set of rays chosen to be parallel to the Cartesian axes of a cubical simulation volume. The hydrodynamical simulations were carried out using a uniform Cartesian grid and the rays were chosen so that each grid cell was sampled by three rays (one per direction). Since the attenuation was modelled in both directions along a ray, the result is that each grid cell sees six different values for the local strength of the radiation field, each one corresponding to 2\u03c0/3 steradians on the sky.\n\n5.62 in the NGC7023 NW PDR by Witt et al. 2006), hinting at increased grain sizes compared to the diffuse ISM. However, we must emphasize that dust properties pertinent to the diffuse ISM (such as the Draine and Li 2007 and Compi\u00e8gne et al. 2011 models) remain used in most PDR models. Models indicate that the grains remain bare until a visual extinction of \u2273 3 (Esplugues et al., 2016; Hollenbach et al., 2009), so that bare surfaces are most relevant for understanding H2 formation in PDRs. Dust grains in the PDR are heated by the UV field and their temperature decreases from the edge to the inner part of the cloud. The grain temperature remains significantly lower than the gas temperature in most PDRs. For large grains, temperatures derived from modified blackbody fits and radiative transfer model are given in Table 2. In high illumination PDRs (such as the Orion Bar, Arab et al., 2012), dust temperatures are found to decrease from 70 K to 35 K, with values of ...\n\nDue to the strong UV fields and gas density variations associated with PDRs, the nature of the dust in these regions evolves from the edge of the PDR to its center, as well as varying from one PDR to another (e.g., Rapacioli et al., 2005, 2006; Bern\u00e9 et al., 2007; Pilleri et al., 2012; Arab et al., 2012; Arab, 2012; K\u00f6hler et al., 2014a; Pilleri et al., 2015). Studies involving the whole dust emission spectrum, from the infrared to the (sub-)mm, indicate significant variations of the dust properties compared with that in the diffuse ISM (e.g., Arab et al., 2012; Arab, 2012). In particular, a decrease in the abundance of small polyaromatic rich carbon grains (denoted as PAHs) by at least a factor of 2 is very likely. This reduction could result from photo-destruction due to the strong UV radiative energy input. Moreover, Pilleri et al. (2012) found that the fraction of carbon locked in very small grains, relative to the total carbon in the IR band carriers (i.e., very small grains and P\n\nPhotodissociation regions (PDRs) are defined as regions in the interstellar medium where the physical and chemical processes are influenced by the ultraviolet radiation field, leading to significant variations in species abundances, thermal balance, and radiative transfer. These regions include diffuse clouds, giant molecular clouds, and environments in active galaxies, and are modeled using advanced computer codes that account for various physical and chemical effects.         "text": "HAL Id: hal-03730888\r\nhttps://hal.science/hal-03730888v1\r\nSubmitted on 6 Sep 2022\r\nHAL is a multi-disciplinary open access\r\narchive for the deposit and dissemination of sci\u0002entific research documents, whether they are pub\u0002lished or not. R\u00f6llig1,4, N. P. Abel2, T. Bell3,17, F. Bensch1, J. Black15, G. J. Ferland2, B. Jonkheid5, I. Kamp6, M. J. Kaufman7,\r\nJ. Le Bourlot8, F. Le Petit8,15, R. Meijerink5, O. Morata16, V. Wolfire14\r\n1 Argelander-Institut f\u00fcr Astronomie\u0001\u0001, Universit\u00e4t Bonn, Auf dem H\u00fcgel 71, 53121 Bonn, Germany\r\ne-mail: roellig@ph1.uni-koeln.de 2 University of Kentucky, Department of Physics and Astronomy, Lexington, KY 40506, USA 3 Department of Physics & Astronomy, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK 4 I. Circulaire, 3 - Ringlaan 3, 1180 Brussel, Belgium 14 Astronomy Department, University of Maryland, College Park, MD 20742-2421, USA 15 Onsala Space Observatory, 439 92 Onsala, Sweden 16 LAEFF, Villafranca del Castillo, Apdo. 50727, 28080 Madrid, Spain 17 California Institute of Technology, 1200 E. California Blvd, Pasadena CA 91125, USA\r\nReceived 27 June 2006 / Accepted 2 February 2007\r\nABSTRACT\r\nAims. We present a comparison between independent computer codes, modeling the physics and chemistry of interstellar photon\r\ndominated regions (PDRs). Our goal was to understand the mutual differences in the PDR codes and their effects on the physical and\r\nchemical structure of the model clouds, and to converge the output of different codes to a common solution.\r\nMethods. A number of benchmark models have been created, covering low and high gas densities n = 103, 105.5 cm\u22123 and far\r\nultraviolet intensities \u03c7 = 10, 105 in units of the Draine field (FUV: 6 < h \u03bd < 13.6 eV). The benchmark models were computed in\r\ntwo ways: one set assuming constant temperatures, thus testing the consistency of the chemical network and photo-processes, and a\r\nsecond set determining the temperature self consistently by solving the thermal balance, thus testing the modeling of the heating and\r\ncooling mechanisms accounting for the detailed energy balance throughout the clouds.\r\nResults. We investigated the impact of PDR geometry and agreed on the comparison of results from spherical and plane-parallel\r\nPDR models. We identified a number of key processes governing the chemical network which have been treated differently in the\r\nvarious codes such as the effect of PAHs on the electron density or the temperature dependence of the dissociation of CO by cosmic\r\nray induced secondary photons, and defined a proper common treatment. We established a comprehensive set of reference models\r\nfor ongoing and future PDR model bench-marking and were able to increase the agreement in model predictions for all benchmark\r\nmodels significantly. Nevertheless, the remaining spread in the computed observables such as the atomic fine-structure line intensities\r\nserves as a warning that there is still a considerable uncertainty when interpreting astronomical data with our models.\r\nKey words. ISM: abundances \u2013 astrochemistry \u2013 ISM: clouds \u2013 ISM: general \u2013 radiative transfer \u2013 methods: numerical\r\n1. Introduction\r\nInterstellar photon dominated regions or photodissociation re\u0002gions (PDRs) play an important role in modern astrophysics\r\nas they are responsible for many emission characteristics of\r\nthe ISM, and dominate the infrared and sub-millimetre spectra\r\nof star formation regions and galaxies as a whole. Theoretical\r\nmodels addressing the structure of PDRs have been available\r\nfor approximately 30 years and have evolved into advanced\r\n\u0001 Appendix A is only available in electronic form at\r\nhttp://www.aanda.org\r\n\u0001\u0001 Founded by merging of the Sternwarte, Radiastronomisches Institut\r\nand Institut f\u00fcr Astrophysik und Extraterestrische Forschung.\r\ncomputer codes accounting for a growing number of physical\r\neffects with increasing accuracy. These codes have been devel\u0002oped with different goals in mind: some are geared to efficiently\r\nmodel a particular type of region, e.g. HII regions, protoplan\u0002etary disks, planetary nebulae, diffuse clouds, etc.; others em\u0002phasize a strict handling of the micro-physical processes in full\r\ndetail (e.g. wavelength dependent absorption), but at the cost of\r\nincreased computing time. Yet others aim at efficient and rapid\r\ncalculation of large model grids for comparison with observa\u0002tional data, which comes at the cost of pragmatic approxima\u0002tions using effective rates rather than detailed treatment. As a\r\nresult, the different models have focused on the detailed simula\u0002tion of particular processes determining the structure in the main\r\nArticle published by EDP Sciences and available at Article published by EDP Sciences and available at Article published by EDP Sciences and available at Article published by EDP Sciences and available at Article published by EDP Sciences and available at http://www.aanda.org http://www.aanda.org http://www.aanda.org http://www.aanda.org http://www.aanda.org or or or or or http://dx.doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361:20065918 http://dx.doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361:20065918 http://dx.doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361:20065918 http://dx.doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361:20065918 http://dx.doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361:20065918\n188 M. R\u00f6llig et al.: A photon dominated region code comparison study\r\nregions of interest while using only rough approximations for\r\nother processes. The model setups vary strongly among different\r\nmodel codes. This includes the assumed model geometry, their\r\nphysical and chemical structure, the choice of free parameters,\r\nand other details. Consequently it is not always straightforward\r\nto directly compare the results from different PDR codes. Taking\r\ninto account that there are multiple ways of implementing physi\u0002cal effects in numerical codes, it is obvious that the model output\r\nof different PDR codes can differ from each other. As a result,\r\nsignificant variations in the physical and chemical PDR struc\u0002ture predicted by the various PDR codes can occur. This diver\u0002gence would prevent a unique interpretation of observed data\r\nin terms of the parameters of the observed clouds. Several new\r\nfacilities such as Herschel, SOFIA, APEX, ALMA, and others\r\nwill become available over the next years and will deliver many\r\nhigh quality observations of line and dust continuum emission\r\nin the sub-millimeter and FIR wavelength regime. Many impor\u0002tant PDR tracers emit in this range ([CII] (158 \u00b5m), [OI] (63 and\r\n146 \u00b5m), [CI] (370 and 610 \u00b5m), CO (650, 520, ..., 57.8 \u00b5m),\r\nH2O, etc.). In order to reliably analyze these data we need a set\r\nof high quality tools, including PDR models that are well un\u0002derstood and properly debugged. As an important preparatory\r\nstep toward these missions an international cooperation between\r\nmany PDR model groups was initiated. The goals of this PDR\u0002benchmarking were:\r\n\u2013 to understand the differences in the different code results;\r\n\u2013 to obtain (as much as possible) the same model output with\r\nevery PDR code when using the same input;\r\n\u2013 to agree on the correct handling of important processes;\r\n\u2013 to identify the specific limits of applicability of the available\r\ncodes.\r\nTo this end, a PDR-benchmarking workshop was held at the\r\nLorentz Center in Leiden, Netherlands in 2004 to jointly work\r\non these topics1. In this paper we present the results from this\r\nworkshop and the results originating from the follow-up activi\u0002ties. A related workshop to test line radiative transfer codes was\r\nheld in 1999 (see van Zadelhoff et al. 2002).\r\nIt is not the purpose of the benchmarking to present a pre\u0002ferred solution or a preferred code. PDRs are found in a large\r\nvariety of objects and under very different conditions. To this\r\nend, it was neither possible nor desirable to develop a generic\r\nPDR code, able to model every possible PDR. Furthermore, the\r\nbenchmarking is not meant to model any \u201creal\u201d astronomical ob\u0002ject. The main purpose of this study is technical not physical.\r\nThis is also reflected in the choice of the adopted incomplete\r\nchemical reaction network (see Sect. 4).\r\nIn Sect. 2 we briefly introduce the physics involved in PDRs,\r\nin Sect. 3 we introduce some key features in PDR modeling.\r\nSection 4 describes the setup of the benchmark calculations and\r\nSect. 5 presents the results for a selection of benchmark calcu\u0002lations and gives a short review over the participating codes. In\r\nSect. 6 we discuss the results and summarize the lessons learned\r\nfrom the benchmark effort. A tabular overview of the individual\r\ncode characteristics is given in the Appendix.\r\n2. The physics of PDRs\r\nPDRs are traditionally defined as regions where H2-non-ionizing\r\nfar-ultraviolet photons from stellar sources control the gas heat\u0002ing and chemistry. Any ionizing radiation is assumed to be ab\u0002sorbed in the narrow ionization fronts located between adjacent\r\n1 URL: http://www.lorentzcenter.nl/\r\nHII regions and the PDRs2. In PDRs the gas is heated by the\r\nfar-ultraviolet radiation (FUV, 6 < h\u03bd < 13.6 eV, from the am\u0002bient UV field and from hot stars) and cooled via the emission of\r\nspectral line radiation of atomic and molecular species and con\u0002tinuum emission by dust (Hollenbach & Tielens 1999; Sternberg\r\n2004). The FUV photons heat the gas by means of photoelec\u0002tric emission from grain surfaces and polycyclic aromatic hy\u0002drocarbons (PAHs) and by collisional de-excitation of vibra\u0002tionally excited H2 molecules. Additional contributions to the\r\ntotal gas heating comes from H2 formation, dissociation of H2,\r\ndust-gas collisions in case of dust temperatures exceeding the\r\ngas temperature, cosmic ray heating, turbulence, and from chem\u0002ical heating. At low visual extinction AV into the cloud/PDR the\r\ngas is cooled by emission of atomic fine-structure lines, mainly\r\n[OI] 63 \u00b5m and [CII] 158 \u00b5m. At larger depths, millimeter,\r\nsub-millimeter and far-infrared molecular rotational-line cool\u0002ing (CO, OH, H2, H2O) becomes important, and a correct treat\u0002ment of the radiative transfer in the line cooling is critical. The\r\nbalance between heating and cooling determines the local gas\r\ntemperature. The local FUV intensity also influences the chemi\u0002cal structure, i.e. the abundance of the individual chemical con\u0002stituents of the gas. The surface of PDRs is mainly dominated by\r\nreactions induced by UV photons, especially the ionization and\r\ndissociation of atoms and molecules. With diminishing FUV in\u0002tensity at higher optical depths more complex species may be\r\nformed without being radiatively destroyed immediately. Thus\r\nthe overall structure of a PDR is the result of a complex inter\u0002play between radiative transfer, energy balance, and chemical\r\nreactions.\r\n3. Modeling of PDRs\r\nThe history of PDR modeling dates back to the early 1970\u2019s\r\n(Hollenbach et al. 1971; Jura 1974; Glassgold & Langer 1975;\r\nBlack & Dalgarno 1977) with steady state models for the tran\u0002sitions from H to H2 and from C+ to CO. In the following\r\nyears a number of models, addressing the chemical and thermal\r\nstructure of clouds subject to an incident flux of FUV photons\r\nhave been developed (de Jong et al. 1980; Tielens & Hollenbach\r\n1985; van Dishoeck & Black 1988; Sternberg & Dalgarno 1989;\r\nHollenbach et al. 1991; Le Bourlot et al. 1993; St\u00f6rzer et al.\r\n1996). Additionally, a number of models, focusing on certain as\u0002pects of PDR physics and chemistry were developed, e.g. mod\u0002els accounting for time-dependent chemical networks, models of\r\nclumped media, and turbulent PDR models (Hill & Hollenbach\r\n1978; Wagenblast & Hartquist 1988; de Boisanger et al. 1992;\r\nBertoldi & Draine 1996; Lee et al. 1996; Hegmann & Kegel\r\n1996; Spaans 1996; Nejad & Wagenblast 1999; R\u00f6llig et al.\r\n2002; Bell et al. 2005). Standard PDR models generally do not\r\naccount for dynamical properties of gas but there are some stud\u0002ies that consider the advection problem rather than the steady\r\nstate approach (e.g. St\u00f6rzer & Hollenbach 1998). For a more\r\ndetailed review see Hollenbach & Tielens (1999).\r\nIn order to numerically model a PDR it is necessary to com\u0002pute all local properties of a cloud such as the relative abun\u0002dances of the gas constituents together with their level popula\u0002tions, temperature of gas and dust, gas pressure, composition of\r\ndust/PAHs, and many more. This local treatment is complicated\r\nby the radiation field which couples remote parts of the cloud.\r\nThe local mean radiation field, which is responsible for photo\u0002chemical reactions, gas/dust heating, and excitation of molecules\r\n2 This distinction is clearer when referring to PDRs as Photo\u0002Dissociation Regions, since molecules are hardly found in HII regions.\nM. R\u00f6llig et al.: A photon dominated region code comparison study 189\r\ndepends on the position in the cloud and the (wavelength depen\u0002dent) absorption along the lines of sight toward this position.\r\nThis non-local coupling makes numerical PDR calculations a\r\nCPU time consuming task.\r\nPDR modelers and observers approach the PDRs from oppo\u0002site sides: PDR models start by calculating the local properties\r\nof the clouds such as the local CO density and the correspond\u0002ing gas temperature and use these local properties to infer the\r\nexpected global properties of the cloud like total emergent emis\u0002sivities or fluxes and column densities. The observer on the other\r\nhand starts by observing global features of a source and tries to\r\ninfer the local properties from that. The connection between lo\u0002cal and global properties is complex and not necessarily unam\u0002biguous. Large variations e.g. in the CO density at the surface\r\nof the cloud may hardly affect the overall CO column density\r\ndue to the dominance of the central part of the cloud with a high\r\ndensity. If one is interested in the total column density it does\r\nnot matter whether different codes produce a different surface\r\nCO density. For the interpretation of high-J CO emission lines,\r\nhowever, different CO densities in the outer cloud layers make a\r\nhuge difference since high temperatures are required to produce\r\nhigh-J CO fluxes. Thus, if different PDR model codes deviate in\r\ntheir predicted cloud structures, this may affect the interpretation\r\nof observations and may prevent inference of the \u201ctrue\u201d struc\u0002ture behind the observed data. To this end it is very important to\r\nunderstand the origin of present differences in PDR model cal\u0002culations. Otherwise it is impossible to rule out alternative inter\u0002pretations. The ideal situation, from the modelers point of view,\r\nwould be a complete knowledge of the true local structure of\r\na real cloud and their global observable properties. This would\r\neasily allow us to calibrate PDR models. Since this case is unob\u0002tainable, we take one step back and apply a different approach:\r\nIf all PDR model codes use exactly the same input and the same\r\nmodel assumptions they should produce the same predictions.\r\nBecause of the close interaction between chemical and ther\u0002mal balance and radiative transfer, PDR codes typically iter\u0002ate through the following computation steps: 1) solve the local\r\nchemical balance to determine local densities; 2) solve the local\r\nenergy balance to estimate the local physical properties like tem\u0002peratures, pressures, and level populations; 3) solve the radiative\r\ntransfer; 4) for finite models it is necessary to successively it\u0002erate steps 1)\u20133). Each step requires a variety of assumptions\r\nand simplifications. Each of these aspects can be investigated to\r\ngreat detail and complexity (see for example van Zadelhoff et al.\r\n(2002) for a discussion of NLTE radiative transfer methods), but\r\nthe explicit aim of the PDR comparison workshop was to under\u0002stand the interaction of all computation steps mentioned above.\r\nEven so it was necessary to considerably reduce the model com\u0002plexity in order to disentangle cause and effect.\r\n3.1. Description of sensitivities and pitfalls\r\nSeveral aspects of PDR modeling have shown the need for de\u0002tailed discussion, easily resulting in misleading conclusions if\r\nnot treated properly.\r\n3.1.1. Model geometry\r\nTwo common geometrical setups of model PDRs are shown in\r\nFig. 1. Most PDR models feature a plane-parallel geometry, il\u0002luminated either from one side or from both sides. This geom\u0002etry naturally suggests a directed illumination, perpendicular to\r\nthe cloud surface. This simplifies the radiative transfer problem\r\nFig. 1. Common geometrical setups of a model PDR. The surface of any\r\nplane-parallel or spherical cloud is illuminated either a) uni-directional\r\nor b) isotropically.\r\nsignificantly, since it is sufficient to account for just one line of\r\nsight, if we ignore scattering out of the line of sight (Flannery\r\net al. 1980). Since most plane-parallel PDR models are infinite\r\nperpendicular to the cloud depth z it is also straightforward to\r\naccount for an isotropic FUV irradiation within the pure 1-D for\u0002malism. For a spherical geometry one can exploit the model\r\nsymmetry only for a FUV field isotropically impinging onto\r\nthe cloud. In finite plane-parallel and spherical models iterations\r\nover the depth/radial structure are mandatory because radiation\r\nis coming from multiple directions, passing through cloud ele\u0002ments for which the physical and chemical structure and hence\r\nopacities have not been calculated in the same iteration step. To\r\naccount for this \u201cbackside\u201d illumination it is essential to iterate\r\non the radiation field.\r\nThe most important quantity describing the radiation field\r\nin PDR models is the local mean intensity (or alternatively the\r\nenergy density) as given by:\r\nJ\u03bd = 1\r\n4 \u03c0\r\n\u0001\r\nI\u03bd d\u2126 [erg cm\u22122 s\r\n\u22121 Hz\u22121 sr\u22121\r\n] (1)\r\nwith the specific intensity I\u03bd being averaged over the solid an\u0002gle \u2126. Note that when referring to the ambient FUV in units of\r\nDraine \u03c7 (Draine 1978) or Habing G0 (Habing 1968) fields, these\r\nare always given as averaged over 4\u03c0. If we place a model cloud\r\nof sufficient optical thickness in such an average FUV field, the\r\nresulting local mean intensity at the cloud edge is half the value\r\nof that without the cloud.\r\nThe choice between directed and isotropic FUV fields\r\ndirectly influences the attenuation due to dust. In the uni\u0002directional case the FU",

Theoretical models addressing the structure of PDRs have been available for approximately 30 years and have evolved into advanced computer codes accounting for a growing number of physical effects with increasing accuracy \u00b7 These codes have been developed with different goals in mind: some are geared to efficiently model a particular type of region, e.g. HII regions, protoplanetary disks, planetary nebulae, diffuse clouds, etc.; others emphasize a strict handling of the micro-physical processes in full detail (e.g. wavelength dependent absorption), but at the cost of increased computing time. The complexity of the model calculations ...\n\nA total of 8 different model clouds were agreed upon for the benchmark comparison. The density and FUV parameter space is covered exemplary by accounting for low and high densities and FUV fields under isothermal conditions, giving 4 different model clouds. The complexity of the model calculations was reduced by setting the gas and dust temperatures to a given constant value (models F1-F4, \u2019F\u2019 denoting a fixed temperature), making the results independent of the solution of the local energy balance. In a second benchmark set, the thermal balance has been solved explicitly thus determining the temperature profile of the cloud (models V1-V4, \u2019V\u2019 denoting variable temperatures).         "url": "https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/full_html/2022/08/aa41854-21/aa41854-21.html",

        "text": "Because of the high model complexity, the large variety in model assumptions, and numerical approximations, it remains difficult to compare results of different PDR model codes. The PDR benchmark was a first attempt to cross-calibrate different PDR model codes against each other (R\u00f6llig et al. 2007). The KOSMA-\u03c4 model is one of the models that participated in the benchmark study. It is the only PDR model employing a spherical model geometry and it has been applied to a large variety of studies in the last 20 yr. PDR models are constantly improved and calibrated against observations of Galactic and extragalactic PDRs. Major constraints were obtained in recent years by a growing number of observations of the main PDR cooling lines, in particular the fine-structure lines of [C II] and [O I]. Modern instruments such as (up)GREAT (German REceiver for Astronomy at Terahertz Frequencies Heyminck et al.\n\nNumerical models of photodissociation regions (PDRs) are a valuable tool to model their chemical and thermodynamic structure and to simulate their line and continuum emission (Tielens & Hollenbach 1985; Hollenbach et al. 1991; Le Bourlot et al. 1993a; Sternberg & Dalgarno 1995; Kaufman et al. 1999; Le Petit et al. 2006; R\u00f6llig et al. 2007, 2013). This is particularly relevant since it is commonly assumed that all neutral atomic hydrogen gas and a large fraction of the molecular gas in galaxies lie in PDRs (Hollenbach & Tielens 1997). The transition from the atomic to the molecular phase in the interstellar medium (ISM) is controlled by the balance between photo-dissociation by far ultraviolet (FUV) radiation and the recombination of atomic hydrogen on grain surfaces. Due to its nature as a line absorption process (Federman et al. 1979), photo-dissociation of H2 is subject to efficient shielding that leads to a sharp transition from H to H2.\n\nBecause of the high model complexity, the large variety in model assumptions, and numerical approximations, it remains difficult to compare results of different PDR model codes. The PDR benchmark was a first attempt to cross-calibrate different PDR model codes against each other (R\u00f6llig et al. 2007). The KOSMA-\u03c4 model is one of the models that participated in the benchmark study. It is the only PDR model employing a spherical model geometry and it has been applied to a large variety of studies in the last 20 yr. PDR models are constantly improved and calibrated against observations of Galactic and extragalactic PDRs. Major constraints were obtained in recent years by a growing number of observations of the main PDR cooling lines, in particular the fine-structure lines of [C II] and [O I]. Modern instruments such as (up)GREAT (German REceiver for Astronomy at Terahertz Frequencies Heyminck et al.\n\nSimilar findings have been reported for the Spica nebula (Choi et al. 2013), the Orion-Eridanus Superbubble (Jo et al. 2011, 2012), and the Taurus-Perseus-Auriga Complex (Lim et al. 2013). A similar scattering effect has been proposed for diffuse H\u03b1 emission outside of bright H II regions (Seon & Witt 2012). Therefore the assumption of an isotropic FUV illumination is a good approximation for low to intermediate mass (size) clumps. It becomes questionable for very large model clumps where the radiation would have to be redistributed over parsec scales. However, the very massive clumps are actually a good approximation to the common plane-parallel setup. In R\u00f6llig et al. (2007) we could show that for these cases our spherical model agrees well with predictions from plane-parallel PDR codes with a perpendicular FUV irradiation producing comparable PDR structures.\n\nBased on measurements in the diffuse medium a mean H2 formation rate of 3 \u00d7 10\u221217 cm3 s\u22121 was found (Jura 1974). This formation rate or comparable temperature dependent parametrizations (e.g., Tielens & Hollenbach 1985; Sternberg & Dalgarno 1995) was implemented in astrochemical models as gas phase reaction simulating the surface process. Later, other prescriptions have been suggested to simulate the formation of H2 more precisely, including considering Langmuir\u2013Hinshelwood and Eley\u2013Rideal processes (e.g., Le Bourlot et al. 2012), physisorption and chemisorption of H2 (Cazaux & Tielens 2002, 2004), as well as stochastic treatment of dust temperatures and grain populations (e.g., Barzel & Biham 2007; Bron et al. 2014). KOSMA-\u03c4 implements the H2 formation formalism described by Cazaux & Tielens (2002) and Cazaux & Tielens (2004). Details are described in R\u00f6llig et al.",

        "title": "F-score - Wikipedia",
        "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/F-score",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "wikipedia.org",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=wikipedia.org",
          "site_name": "Wikipedia"

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Earlier works focused primarily on the F1 score, but with the proliferation of large scale search engines, performance goals changed to place more emphasis on either precision or recall and so ... The F-score is also used in machine learning. However, the F-measures do not take true negatives into account, hence measures such as the Matthews correlation coefficient, Informedness or Cohen&#x27;s kappa may be preferred to assess the performance of a binary classifier. The F-score has been widely used in the natural language processing literature, such as in the evaluation of named entity recognition and word segmentation. The F1 score is the Dice coefficient of the set of retrieved items and the set of relevant items. The F1-score of a classifier which always predicts the positive class converges to 1 as the probability of the positive class increases. The F1-score of a classifier which always predicts the positive class is equal to 2 * proportion_of_positive_class / ( 1 + proportion_of_posit\n\nEarlier works focused primarily on the F1 score, but with the proliferation of large scale search engines, performance goals changed to place more emphasis on either precision or recall and so ... The F-score is also used in machine learning. However, the F-measures do not take true negatives into account, hence measures such as the Matthews correlation coefficient, Informedness or Cohen's kappa may be preferred to assess the performance of a binary classifier. The F-score has been widely used in the natural language processing literature, such as in the evaluation of named entity recognition and word segmentation. The F1 score is the Dice coefficient of the set of retrieved items and the set of relevant items. The F1-score of a classifier which always predicts the positive class converges to 1 as the probability of the positive class increases. The F1-score of a classifier which always predicts the positive class is equal to 2 * proportion_of_positive_class / ( 1 + proportion_of_posit\n\nDavid M W Powers has pointed out that F1 ignores the True Negatives and thus is misleading for unbalanced classes, while kappa and correlation measures are symmetric and assess both directions of predictability - the classifier predicting the true class and the true class predicting the classifier prediction, proposing separate multiclass measures Informedness and Markedness for the two directions, noting that their geometric mean is correlation. Another source of critique of F1 is its lack of symmetry. It means it may change its value when dataset labeling is changed - the \"positive\" samples are named \"negative\" and vice versa. This criticism is met by the P4 metric definition, which is sometimes indicated as a symmetrical extension of F1. Finally, Ferrer and Dyrland et al. argue that the expected cost (or its counterpart, the expected utility) is the only principled metric for evaluation of classification decisions, having various advantages over the F-score and the MCC.\n\nF1 score is concave in the true positive rate. David Hand and others criticize the widespread use of the F1 score since it gives equal importance to precision and recall. In practice, different types of mis-classifications incur different costs. In other words, the relative importance of precision and recall is an aspect of the problem. According to Davide Chicco and Giuseppe Jurman, the F1 score is less truthful and informative than the Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) in binary evaluation classification.\n\nMacro F1 is a macro-averaged F1 score aiming at a balanced performance measurement. To calculate macro F1, two different averaging-formulas have been used: the F1 score of (arithmetic) class-wise precision and recall means or the arithmetic mean of class-wise F1 scores, where the latter exhibits more desirable properties. Micro F1 is the harmonic mean of micro precision and micro recall. In single-label multi-class classification, micro precision equals micro recall, thus micro F1 is equal to both.\n\nThe F1 score is the harmonic mean of the precision and recall. It thus symmetrically represents both precision and recall in one metric. The more generic ... {\\displaystyle F_{\\beta }} score applies additional weights, valuing one of precision or recall more than the other. The highest possible value of an F-score is 1.0, indicating perfect precision and recall, and the lowest possible value is 0, if the precision or the recall is zero. The name F-measure is believed to be named after a different F function in Van Rijsbergen's book, when introduced to the Fourth Message Understanding Conference (MUC-4, 1992). The traditional F-measure or balanced F-score (F1 score) is the harmonic mean of precision and recall: ...",

          "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/F-score",

          "age": "January 21, 2026"

      "file_text": "\"\"\"\nR\u00f6llig+ (2007) PDR Benchmark Suite for PRISM-3D.\n\nImplements the 8 standard benchmark models from:\n  \"A photon dominated region code comparison study\"\n  R\u00f6llig, Abel, Bell, Bensch, Black, et al.\n  A&A 467, 187-206 (2007)\n\nBenchmark models:\n  F1-F4: Fixed (isothermal) temperature, tests chemistry + RT only\n  V1-V4: Variable temperature, tests full thermal balance\n\n  Model   n [cm\u207b\u00b3]   \u03c7 [Draine]   T_gas [K]   Notes\n  \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n  F1      10\u00b3        10           50          Low n, low \u03c7\n  F2      10\u00b3        10\u2075          50          Low n, high \u03c7\n  F3      10\u2075\u00b7\u2075      10           50          High n, low \u03c7\n  F4      10\u2075\u00b7\u2075      10\u2075          50          High n, high \u03c7\n  V1      10\u00b3        10           solved      Low n, low \u03c7\n  V2      10\u00b3        10\u2075          solved      Low n, high \u03c7\n  V3      10\u2075\u00b7\u2075      10           solved      High n, low \u03c7\n  V4      10\u2075\u00b7\u2075      10\u2075          solved      High n, high \u03c7\n\nStandard benchmark conventions (R\u00f6llig+ 2007, Sect. 4):\n  - \u03c7 is in Draine (1978) field units: \u03c7=1 \u2192 F_FUV = 2.7e-3 erg/cm\u00b2/s\n  - G\u2080 (Habing) = \u03c7 / 1.71 (conversion factor)\n  - Plane-parallel semi-infinite slab, illuminated from one side\n  - Constant density (no density gradient)\n  - Total depth: A_V = 10 mag\n  - Cosmic ray ionization rate: \u03b6 = 5e-17 s\u207b\u00b9\n  - Solar elemental abundances (see below)\n  - Standardized chemical network: H, He, C, O, S, Si, Fe + electrons\n  - Results plotted vs A_V,eff (effective visual extinction from surface)\n\nStandardized abundances (gas-phase, relative to n_H):\n  He: 0.1,  C: 1.4e-4,  O: 3.0e-4,  S: 2.8e-5,\n  Si: 1.7e-6, Fe: 1.7e-7, N: 7.5e-5\n  (Note: these are the BENCHMARK abundances, not solar or depleted)\n\nKey comparables:\n  - T(A_V) profiles (V models)\n  - n(H), n(H2), n(C+), n(C), n(CO) vs A_V\n  - Surface [CII] 158 \u03bcm, [OI] 63 \u03bcm intensities\n  - CO column densities\n\"\"\"\n\nimport numpy as np\nimport sys\nimport os\nimport time\n\n# Ensure our package is importable\nsys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))))\n\n\n# ============================================================\n# R\u00f6llig benchmark abundances\n# ============================================================\nROELLIG_ABUNDANCES = {\n    'He': 0.1,\n    'C':  1.4e-4,\n    'O':  3.0e-4,\n    'N':  7.5e-5,\n    'S':  2.8e-5,\n    'Si': 1.7e-6,\n    'Fe': 1.7e-7,\n}\n\n# Draine \u2192 Habing conversion\nCHI_TO_G0 = 1.0 / 1.71   # G0 = chi / 1.71\n\n# Benchmark CR ionization rate\nZETA_CR_BENCHMARK = 5e-17  # s\u207b\u00b9\n\n# ============================================================\n# Benchmark model definitions\n# ============================================================\nBENCHMARK_MODELS = {\n    'F1': {'n_H': 1e3,      'chi': 10,    'T_fixed': 50.0,  'variable_T': False},\n    'F2': {'n_H': 1e3,      'chi': 1e5,   'T_fixed': 50.0,  'variable_T': False},\n    'F3': {'n_H': 10**5.5,  'chi': 10,    'T_fixed': 50.0,  'variable_T': False},\n    'F4': {'n_H': 10**5.5,  'chi': 1e5,   'T_fixed': 50.0,  'variable_T': False},\n    'V1': {'n_H': 1e3,      'chi': 10,    'T_fixed': None,   'variable_T': True},\n    'V2': {'n_H': 1e3,      'chi': 1e5,   'T_fixed': None,   'variable_T': True},\n    'V3': {'n_H': 10**5.5,  'chi': 10,    'T_fixed': None,   'variable_T': True},\n    'V4': {'n_H': 10**5.5,  'chi': 1e5,   'T_fixed': None,   'variable_T': True},\n}\n\n\ndef setup_benchmark_model(model_name, n_cells=32, use_3d_rt=False, nside_rt=2):\n    \"\"\"\n    Set up a R\u00f6llig benchmark model.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    model_name : str\n        One of: F1, F2, F3, F4, V1, V2, V3, V4\n    n_cells : int\n        Number of cells along the slab depth.\n    use_3d_rt : bool\n        If True, use full 3D HEALPix ray tracing.\n        If False, use fast 1D column density integration.\n    nside_rt : int\n        HEALPix resolution (only used if use_3d_rt=True).\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    solver : PDRSolver\n        Configured solver ready to run.\n    model_params : dict\n        Model parameters for reference.\n    \"\"\"\n    from prism3d.core.grid import OctreeGrid, OctreeNode, CellData\n    from prism3d.core.solver import PDRSolver\n    from prism3d.utils.constants import AV_per_NH, NH_per_AV\n\n    if model_name not in BENCHMARK_MODELS:\n        raise ValueError(f\"Unknown model: {model_name}. \"\n                        f\"Choose from: {list(BENCHMARK_MODELS.keys())}\")\n\n    params = BENCHMARK_MODELS[model_name].copy()\n    n_H = params['n_H']\n    chi = params['chi']\n    G0 = chi * CHI_TO_G0\n    T_fixed = params['T_fixed']\n    variable_T = params['variable_T']\n\n    # Slab depth: A_V = 10 mag\n    AV_max = 10.0\n    N_H_total = AV_max * NH_per_AV\n    depth = N_H_total / n_H  # cm\n\n    print(f\"\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\")\n    print(f\"  PRISM-3D R\u00f6llig Benchmark: Model {model_name}\")\n    print(f\"\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\")\n    print(f\"  n_H  = {n_H:.2e} cm\u207b\u00b3\")\n    print(f\"  \u03c7    = {chi:.0e} (Draine) \u2192 G\u2080 = {G0:.1f} (Habing)\")\n    print(f\"  \u03b6_CR = {ZETA_CR_BENCHMARK:.1e} s\u207b\u00b9\")\n    print(f\"  A_V  = 0 \u2192 {AV_max} mag\")\n    print(f\"  Depth = {depth:.2e} cm = {depth/3.086e18:.3f} pc\")\n    if T_fixed is not None:\n        print(f\"  T_gas = {T_fixed} K (FIXED)\")\n    else:\n        print(f\"  T_gas = solved self-consistently\")\n    print(f\"  Cells = {n_cells}\")\n    print(f\"  3D RT = {use_3d_rt} (nside={nside_rt})\")\n    print(f\"\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\")\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 Build 1D grid \u2500\u2500\n    cell_size = depth / n_cells\n    grid = OctreeGrid(box_size=depth, n_base=1, max_level=0)\n    grid.root_nodes = []\n    half_depth = depth / 2.0\n\n    for i in range(n_cells):\n        center = np.array([-half_depth + (i + 0.5) * cell_size, 0.0, 0.0])\n        node = OctreeNode(center, cell_size, level=0)\n        node.data = CellData()\n        node.data.n_H = n_H\n        grid.root_nodes.append(node)\n\n    grid.n_base = 1\n    grid.base_cell_size = depth\n    grid.invalidate_cache()\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 Initialize with benchmark abundances \u2500\u2500\n    x_C = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['C']\n    x_O = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['O']\n    x_S = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['S']\n    x_Si = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['Si']\n    x_Fe = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['Fe']\n\n    for i, node in enumerate(grid.root_nodes):\n        x = (i + 0.5) * cell_size\n        N_H_col = n_H * x\n        node.data.A_V = N_H_col * AV_per_NH\n        node.data.G0 = G0 * np.exp(-1.8 * node.data.A_V)\n        node.data.N_H_total = N_H_col\n        A = node.data.A_V\n\n        # Initial conditions based on depth\n        if A < 0.5:\n            node.data.x_HI = 1.0; node.data.x_H2 = 1e-6\n            node.data.x_Cp = x_C; node.data.x_C = 1e-10; node.data.x_CO = 1e-15\n            node.data.x_O = x_O\n            node.data.T_gas = T_fixed if T_fixed else 500.0\n        elif A < 2.0:\n            f = (A - 0.5) / 1.5\n            node.data.x_HI = 1.0 - f; node.data.x_H2 = f / 2.0\n            node.data.x_Cp = x_C * (1 - 0.5 * f)\n            node.data.x_C = x_C * 0.3 * f\n            node.data.x_CO = x_C * 0.2 * f\n            node.data.x_O = x_O - node.data.x_CO\n            node.data.T_gas = T_fixed if T_fixed else 200.0\n        elif A < 5.0:\n            fCO = (A - 2.0) / 3.0\n            node.data.x_HI = 0.01; node.data.x_H2 = 0.495\n            node.data.x_Cp = max(x_C * (1 - 3 * fCO), 1e-9)\n            node.data.x_C = x_C * (1 - fCO) * 0.3\n            node.data.x_CO = x_C * fCO * 0.9\n            node.data.x_O = x_O - node.data.x_CO\n            node.data.T_gas = T_fixed if T_fixed else 60.0\n        else:\n            node.data.x_HI = 0.005; node.data.x_H2 = 0.4975\n            node.data.x_Cp = 1e-9\n            node.data.x_C = x_C * 0.05\n            node.data.x_CO = x_C * 0.95\n            node.data.x_O = x_O - node.data.x_CO\n            node.data.T_gas = T_fixed if T_fixed else 15.0\n\n        # Metal ions (depleted at depth)\n        node.data.x_Sp = x_S if A < 3 else x_S * 0.01\n        node.data.x_S = x_S - node.data.x_Sp\n        node.data.x_Sip = x_Si if A < 3 else 1e-10\n        node.data.x_Si = x_Si - node.data.x_Sip\n        node.data.x_Fep = x_Fe if A < 3 else 1e-10\n        node.data.x_Fe = x_Fe - node.data.x_Fep\n        node.data.x_e = node.data.x_Cp + node.data.x_Sp + node.data.x_Sip + node.data.x_Fep + 1e-8\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 Create solver \u2500\u2500\n    solver = PDRSolver(\n        grid,\n        G0_external=G0,\n        zeta_CR_0=ZETA_CR_BENCHMARK,\n        use_1d_rt=(not use_3d_rt),\n        nside_rt=nside_rt,\n        cr_model='M'\n    )\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 Override RT with simple 1D for the 1D benchmark \u2500\u2500\n    if not use_3d_rt:\n        solver.rt = _Simple1DRT(grid, G0)\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 For fixed-T models, disable thermal solver \u2500\u2500\n    if T_fixed is not None:\n        solver._fixed_T = T_fixed\n    else:\n        solver._fixed_T = None\n\n    return solver, params\n\n\nclass _Simple1DRT:\n    \"\"\"Fast 1D column density integrator for benchmarking.\"\"\"\n    def __init__(self, grid, G0):\n        self.grid = grid\n        self.G0_external = G0\n\n    def compute_1d_column_densities(self, axis=0, direction=1):\n        from prism3d.utils.constants import AV_per_NH\n        leaves = sorted(self.grid.get_leaves(), key=lambda l: l.center[0])\n        N_H = N_H2 = N_CO = 0.0\n        for leaf in leaves:\n            ds = leaf.size\n            leaf.data.N_H_total = N_H + 0.5 * leaf.data.n_H * ds\n            leaf.data.N_H2 = N_H2 + 0.5 * leaf.data.n_H * leaf.data.x_H2 * 2.0 * ds\n            leaf.data.N_CO = N_CO + 0.5 * leaf.data.n_H * leaf.data.x_CO * ds\n            leaf.data.A_V = leaf.data.N_H_total * AV_per_NH\n            leaf.data.G0 = self.G0_external * np.exp(-1.8 * leaf.data.A_V)\n            N_H += leaf.data.n_H * ds\n            N_H2 += leaf.data.n_H * leaf.data.x_H2 * 2.0 * ds\n            N_CO += leaf.data.n_H * leaf.data.x_CO * ds\n\n    def compute_fuv_field(self):\n        self.compute_1d_column_densities()\n\n\ndef run_benchmark(model_name, n_cells=32, max_iter=10, use_3d_rt=False,\n                  nside_rt=2, convergence_tol=0.05):\n    \"\"\"\n    Run a single R\u00f6llig benchmark model and return results.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    profile : dict\n        Arrays of A_V, T_gas, T_dust, n_H, n(H), n(H2),\n        n(C+), n(C), n(CO), n(O), n(e) vs depth.\n    \"\"\"\n    solver, params = setup_benchmark_model(\n        model_name, n_cells=n_cells,\n        use_3d_rt=use_3d_rt, nside_rt=nside_rt\n    )\n\n    t0 = time.time()\n    converged = solver.run(\n        max_iterations=max_iter,\n        convergence_tol=convergence_tol,\n        verbose=True\n    )\n    elapsed = time.time() - t0\n    print(f\"\\nTotal wall time: {elapsed:.1f}s\")\n\n    # Extract profile\n    leaves = sorted(solver.grid.get_leaves(), key=lambda l: l.center[0])\n    n_H = params['n_H']\n\n    profile = {\n        'model': model_name,\n        'n_H': n_H,\n        'chi': params['chi'],\n        'converged': converged,\n        'wall_time': elapsed,\n        'A_V': np.array([l.data.A_V for l in leaves]),\n        'T_gas': np.array([l.data.T_gas for l in leaves]),\n        'T_dust': np.array([l.data.T_dust for l in leaves]),\n        'G0': np.array([l.data.G0 for l in leaves]),\n        # Densities (not fractions) for R\u00f6llig comparison\n        'n_HI': np.array([l.data.x_HI * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_H2': np.array([l.data.x_H2 * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_Cp': np.array([l.data.x_Cp * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_C':  np.array([l.data.x_C * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_CO': np.array([l.data.x_CO * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_O':  np.array([l.data.x_O * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_e':  np.array([l.data.x_e * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        # Fractions too\n        'x_HI': np.array([l.data.x_HI for l in leaves]),\n        'x_H2': np.array([l.data.x_H2 for l in leaves]),\n        'x_Cp': np.array([l.data.x_Cp for l in leaves]),\n        'x_C':  np.array([l.data.x_C for l in leaves]),\n        'x_CO': np.array([l.data.x_CO for l in leaves]),\n        'x_e':  np.array([l.data.x_e for l in leaves]),\n    }\n\n    return profile\n\n\ndef plot_benchmark_comparison(profiles, save_path=None):\n    \"\"\"\n    Plot benchmark results in the R\u00f6llig+ (2007) format.\n\n    Generates a 2\u00d73 panel figure for each model:\n    - n(H), n(H2) vs A_V\n    - n(C+), n(C), n(CO) vs A_V\n    - T(A_V) for V models\n    - n(e) vs A_V\n    - G0 vs A_V\n    \"\"\"\n    import matplotlib\n    matplotlib.use('Agg')\n    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n\n    n_models = len(profiles)\n    fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 11))\n\n    colors = {'F1': '#1f77b4', 'F2': '#ff7f0e', 'F3': '#2ca02c', 'F4': '#d62728',\n              'V1': '#1f77b4', 'V2': '#ff7f0e', 'V3': '#2ca02c', 'V4': '#d62728'}\n    styles = {'F1': '-', 'F2': '-', 'F3': '-', 'F4': '-',\n              'V1': '--', 'V2': '--', 'V3': '--', 'V4': '--'}\n\n    for p in profiles:\n        name = p['model']\n        A = p['A_V']\n        c = colors.get(name, 'black')\n        ls = styles.get(name, '-')\n        lbl = f\"{name} (n={p['n_H']:.0e}, \u03c7={p['chi']:.0e})\"\n\n        # Panel 1: H/H2\n        ax = axes[0, 0]\n        ax.semilogy(A, p['n_HI'], color=c, ls=ls, lw=1.5, label=f'{name} H')\n        ax.semilogy(A, p['n_H2'], color=c, ls=':', lw=1.5)\n\n        # Panel 2: C+/C/CO\n        ax = axes[0, 1]\n        ax.semilogy(A, p['n_Cp'], color=c, ls=ls, lw=1.5, label=f'{name} C+')\n        ax.semilogy(A, p['n_C'], color=c, ls='--', lw=1.0)\n        ax.semilogy(A, p['n_CO'], color=c, ls=':', lw=1.5)\n\n        # Panel 3: Temperature\n        ax = axes[0, 2]\n        ax.semilogy(A, p['T_gas'], color=c, ls=ls, lw=1.5, label=name)\n\n        # Panel 4: Electron density\n        ax = axes[1, 0]\n        ax.semilogy(A, p['n_e'], color=c, ls=ls, lw=1.5, label=name)\n\n        # Panel 5: FUV field\n        ax = axes[1, 1]\n        ax.semilogy(A, np.maximum(p['G0'], 1e-3), color=c, ls=ls, lw=1.5, label=name)\n\n    # Labels\n    axes[0, 0].set_xlabel('A_V [mag]'); axes[0, 0].set_ylabel('n [cm\u207b\u00b3]')\n    axes[0, 0].set_title('H / H\u2082 densities'); axes[0, 0].legend(fontsize=7)\n    axes[0, 1].set_xlabel('A_V [mag]'); axes[0, 1].set_ylabel('n [cm\u207b\u00b3]')\n    axes[0, 1].set_title('C\u207a (solid) / C (dash) / CO (dot)'); axes[0, 1].legend(fontsize=7)\n    axes[0, 2].set_xlabel('A_V [mag]'); axes[0, 2].set_ylabel('T [K]')\n    axes[0, 2].set_title('Gas Temperature'); axes[0, 2].legend(fontsize=7)\n    axes[1, 0].set_xlabel('A_V [mag]'); axes[1, 0].set_ylabel('n_e [cm\u207b\u00b3]')\n    axes[1, 0].set_title('Electron density'); axes[1, 0].legend(fontsize=7)\n    axes[1, 1].set_xlabel('A_V [mag]'); axes[1, 1].set_ylabel('G\u2080 [Habing]')\n    axes[1, 1].set_title('FUV Field'); axes[1, 1].legend(fontsize=7)\n\n    # Panel 6: Summary table\n    ax = axes[1, 2]\n    ax.axis('off')\n    rows = []\n    for p in profiles:\n        rows.append([\n            p['model'],\n            f\"{p['n_H']:.0e}\",\n            f\"{p['chi']:.0e}\",\n            'Y' if p['converged'] else 'N',\n            f\"{p['wall_time']:.0f}s\",\n        ])\n    table = ax.table(cellText=rows,\n                     colLabels=['Model', 'n_H', '\u03c7', 'Conv', 'Time'],\n                     loc='center', cellLoc='center')\n    table.auto_set_font_size(False)\n    table.set_fontsize(10)\n    table.scale(1, 1.5)\n    ax.set_title('Benchmark Summary')\n\n    fig.suptitle('PRISM-3D: R\u00f6llig+ (2007) PDR Benchmark', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')\n    plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96])\n\n    if save_path:\n        plt.savefig(save_path, dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')\n        print(f\"Plot saved: {save_path}\")\n    plt.close()\n\n\ndef run_all_benchmarks(models=None, n_cells=24, max_iter=8, save_dir=None):\n    \"\"\"\n    Run all (or selected) R\u00f6llig benchmark models.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    models : list, optional\n        List of model names. Default: all 8.\n    \"\"\"\n    if models is None:\n        models = ['F1', 'F2', 'F3', 'F4', 'V1', 'V2', 'V3', 'V4']\n\n    profiles = []\n    for name in models:\n        print(f\"\\n{'='*60}\")\n        print(f\"  Running benchmark model {name}\")\n        print(f\"{'='*60}\\n\")\n        try:\n            p = run_benchmark(name, n_cells=n_cells, max_iter=max_iter)\n            profiles.append(p)\n        except Exception as e:\n            print(f\"  ERROR in model {name}: {e}\")\n\n    if save_dir:\n        plot_benchmark_comparison(\n            profiles,\n            save_path=os.path.join(save_dir, 'roellig_benchmark.png')\n        )\n\n    return profiles\n\n\nif __name__ == '__main__':\n    import argparse\n    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Run R\u00f6llig PDR benchmark')\n    parser.add_argument('--models', nargs='+', default=['V1', 'V2'],\n                       help='Models to run (e.g., F1 F2 V1 V2)')\n    parser.add_argument('--cells', type=int, default=24)\n    parser.add_argument('--iter', type=int, default=8)\n    parser.add_argument('--output', default='/home/claude')\n    args = parser.parse_args()\n\n    profiles = run_all_benchmarks(\n        models=args.models,\n        n_cells=args.cells,\n        max_iter=args.iter,\n        save_dir=args.output\n    )\n",

    "display_content": {
      "type": "code_block",
      "language": "python",
      "code": "\"\"\"\nR\u00f6llig+ (2007) PDR Benchmark Suite for PRISM-3D.\n\nImplements the 8 standard benchmark models from:\n  \"A photon dominated region code comparison study\"\n  R\u00f6llig, Abel, Bell, Bensch, Black, et al.\n  A&A 467, 187-206 (2007)\n\nBenchmark models:\n  F1-F4: Fixed (isothermal) temperature, tests chemistry + RT only\n  V1-V4: Variable temperature, tests full thermal balance\n\n  Model   n [cm\u207b\u00b3]   \u03c7 [Draine]   T_gas [K]   Notes\n  \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500   \u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\n  F1      10\u00b3        10           50          Low n, low \u03c7\n  F2      10\u00b3        10\u2075          50          Low n, high \u03c7\n  F3      10\u2075\u00b7\u2075      10           50          High n, low \u03c7\n  F4      10\u2075\u00b7\u2075      10\u2075          50          High n, high \u03c7\n  V1      10\u00b3        10           solved      Low n, low \u03c7\n  V2      10\u00b3        10\u2075          solved      Low n, high \u03c7\n  V3      10\u2075\u00b7\u2075      10           solved      High n, low \u03c7\n  V4      10\u2075\u00b7\u2075      10\u2075          solved      High n, high \u03c7\n\nStandard benchmark conventions (R\u00f6llig+ 2007, Sect. 4):\n  - \u03c7 is in Draine (1978) field units: \u03c7=1 \u2192 F_FUV = 2.7e-3 erg/cm\u00b2/s\n  - G\u2080 (Habing) = \u03c7 / 1.71 (conversion factor)\n  - Plane-parallel semi-infinite slab, illuminated from one side\n  - Constant density (no density gradient)\n  - Total depth: A_V = 10 mag\n  - Cosmic ray ionization rate: \u03b6 = 5e-17 s\u207b\u00b9\n  - Solar elemental abundances (see below)\n  - Standardized chemical network: H, He, C, O, S, Si, Fe + electrons\n  - Results plotted vs A_V,eff (effective visual extinction from surface)\n\nStandardized abundances (gas-phase, relative to n_H):\n  He: 0.1,  C: 1.4e-4,  O: 3.0e-4,  S: 2.8e-5,\n  Si: 1.7e-6, Fe: 1.7e-7, N: 7.5e-5\n  (Note: these are the BENCHMARK abundances, not solar or depleted)\n\nKey comparables:\n  - T(A_V) profiles (V models)\n  - n(H), n(H2), n(C+), n(C), n(CO) vs A_V\n  - Surface [CII] 158 \u03bcm, [OI] 63 \u03bcm intensities\n  - CO column densities\n\"\"\"\n\nimport numpy as np\nimport sys\nimport os\nimport time\n\n# Ensure our package is importable\nsys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))))\n\n\n# ============================================================\n# R\u00f6llig benchmark abundances\n# ============================================================\nROELLIG_ABUNDANCES = {\n    'He': 0.1,\n    'C':  1.4e-4,\n    'O':  3.0e-4,\n    'N':  7.5e-5,\n    'S':  2.8e-5,\n    'Si': 1.7e-6,\n    'Fe': 1.7e-7,\n}\n\n# Draine \u2192 Habing conversion\nCHI_TO_G0 = 1.0 / 1.71   # G0 = chi / 1.71\n\n# Benchmark CR ionization rate\nZETA_CR_BENCHMARK = 5e-17  # s\u207b\u00b9\n\n# ============================================================\n# Benchmark model definitions\n# ============================================================\nBENCHMARK_MODELS = {\n    'F1': {'n_H': 1e3,      'chi': 10,    'T_fixed': 50.0,  'variable_T': False},\n    'F2': {'n_H': 1e3,      'chi': 1e5,   'T_fixed': 50.0,  'variable_T': False},\n    'F3': {'n_H': 10**5.5,  'chi': 10,    'T_fixed': 50.0,  'variable_T': False},\n    'F4': {'n_H': 10**5.5,  'chi': 1e5,   'T_fixed': 50.0,  'variable_T': False},\n    'V1': {'n_H': 1e3,      'chi': 10,    'T_fixed': None,   'variable_T': True},\n    'V2': {'n_H': 1e3,      'chi': 1e5,   'T_fixed': None,   'variable_T': True},\n    'V3': {'n_H': 10**5.5,  'chi': 10,    'T_fixed': None,   'variable_T': True},\n    'V4': {'n_H': 10**5.5,  'chi': 1e5,   'T_fixed': None,   'variable_T': True},\n}\n\n\ndef setup_benchmark_model(model_name, n_cells=32, use_3d_rt=False, nside_rt=2):\n    \"\"\"\n    Set up a R\u00f6llig benchmark model.\n\n    Parameters\n    ----------\n    model_name : str\n        One of: F1, F2, F3, F4, V1, V2, V3, V4\n    n_cells : int\n        Number of cells along the slab depth.\n    use_3d_rt : bool\n        If True, use full 3D HEALPix ray tracing.\n        If False, use fast 1D column density integration.\n    nside_rt : int\n        HEALPix resolution (only used if use_3d_rt=True).\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    solver : PDRSolver\n        Configured solver ready to run.\n    model_params : dict\n        Model parameters for reference.\n    \"\"\"\n    from prism3d.core.grid import OctreeGrid, OctreeNode, CellData\n    from prism3d.core.solver import PDRSolver\n    from prism3d.utils.constants import AV_per_NH, NH_per_AV\n\n    if model_name not in BENCHMARK_MODELS:\n        raise ValueError(f\"Unknown model: {model_name}. \"\n                        f\"Choose from: {list(BENCHMARK_MODELS.keys())}\")\n\n    params = BENCHMARK_MODELS[model_name].copy()\n    n_H = params['n_H']\n    chi = params['chi']\n    G0 = chi * CHI_TO_G0\n    T_fixed = params['T_fixed']\n    variable_T = params['variable_T']\n\n    # Slab depth: A_V = 10 mag\n    AV_max = 10.0\n    N_H_total = AV_max * NH_per_AV\n    depth = N_H_total / n_H  # cm\n\n    print(f\"\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\")\n    print(f\"  PRISM-3D R\u00f6llig Benchmark: Model {model_name}\")\n    print(f\"\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\")\n    print(f\"  n_H  = {n_H:.2e} cm\u207b\u00b3\")\n    print(f\"  \u03c7    = {chi:.0e} (Draine) \u2192 G\u2080 = {G0:.1f} (Habing)\")\n    print(f\"  \u03b6_CR = {ZETA_CR_BENCHMARK:.1e} s\u207b\u00b9\")\n    print(f\"  A_V  = 0 \u2192 {AV_max} mag\")\n    print(f\"  Depth = {depth:.2e} cm = {depth/3.086e18:.3f} pc\")\n    if T_fixed is not None:\n        print(f\"  T_gas = {T_fixed} K (FIXED)\")\n    else:\n        print(f\"  T_gas = solved self-consistently\")\n    print(f\"  Cells = {n_cells}\")\n    print(f\"  3D RT = {use_3d_rt} (nside={nside_rt})\")\n    print(f\"\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\u2550\")\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 Build 1D grid \u2500\u2500\n    cell_size = depth / n_cells\n    grid = OctreeGrid(box_size=depth, n_base=1, max_level=0)\n    grid.root_nodes = []\n    half_depth = depth / 2.0\n\n    for i in range(n_cells):\n        center = np.array([-half_depth + (i + 0.5) * cell_size, 0.0, 0.0])\n        node = OctreeNode(center, cell_size, level=0)\n        node.data = CellData()\n        node.data.n_H = n_H\n        grid.root_nodes.append(node)\n\n    grid.n_base = 1\n    grid.base_cell_size = depth\n    grid.invalidate_cache()\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 Initialize with benchmark abundances \u2500\u2500\n    x_C = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['C']\n    x_O = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['O']\n    x_S = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['S']\n    x_Si = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['Si']\n    x_Fe = ROELLIG_ABUNDANCES['Fe']\n\n    for i, node in enumerate(grid.root_nodes):\n        x = (i + 0.5) * cell_size\n        N_H_col = n_H * x\n        node.data.A_V = N_H_col * AV_per_NH\n        node.data.G0 = G0 * np.exp(-1.8 * node.data.A_V)\n        node.data.N_H_total = N_H_col\n        A = node.data.A_V\n\n        # Initial conditions based on depth\n        if A < 0.5:\n            node.data.x_HI = 1.0; node.data.x_H2 = 1e-6\n            node.data.x_Cp = x_C; node.data.x_C = 1e-10; node.data.x_CO = 1e-15\n            node.data.x_O = x_O\n            node.data.T_gas = T_fixed if T_fixed else 500.0\n        elif A < 2.0:\n            f = (A - 0.5) / 1.5\n            node.data.x_HI = 1.0 - f; node.data.x_H2 = f / 2.0\n            node.data.x_Cp = x_C * (1 - 0.5 * f)\n            node.data.x_C = x_C * 0.3 * f\n            node.data.x_CO = x_C * 0.2 * f\n            node.data.x_O = x_O - node.data.x_CO\n            node.data.T_gas = T_fixed if T_fixed else 200.0\n        elif A < 5.0:\n            fCO = (A - 2.0) / 3.0\n            node.data.x_HI = 0.01; node.data.x_H2 = 0.495\n            node.data.x_Cp = max(x_C * (1 - 3 * fCO), 1e-9)\n            node.data.x_C = x_C * (1 - fCO) * 0.3\n            node.data.x_CO = x_C * fCO * 0.9\n            node.data.x_O = x_O - node.data.x_CO\n            node.data.T_gas = T_fixed if T_fixed else 60.0\n        else:\n            node.data.x_HI = 0.005; node.data.x_H2 = 0.4975\n            node.data.x_Cp = 1e-9\n            node.data.x_C = x_C * 0.05\n            node.data.x_CO = x_C * 0.95\n            node.data.x_O = x_O - node.data.x_CO\n            node.data.T_gas = T_fixed if T_fixed else 15.0\n\n        # Metal ions (depleted at depth)\n        node.data.x_Sp = x_S if A < 3 else x_S * 0.01\n        node.data.x_S = x_S - node.data.x_Sp\n        node.data.x_Sip = x_Si if A < 3 else 1e-10\n        node.data.x_Si = x_Si - node.data.x_Sip\n        node.data.x_Fep = x_Fe if A < 3 else 1e-10\n        node.data.x_Fe = x_Fe - node.data.x_Fep\n        node.data.x_e = node.data.x_Cp + node.data.x_Sp + node.data.x_Sip + node.data.x_Fep + 1e-8\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 Create solver \u2500\u2500\n    solver = PDRSolver(\n        grid,\n        G0_external=G0,\n        zeta_CR_0=ZETA_CR_BENCHMARK,\n        use_1d_rt=(not use_3d_rt),\n        nside_rt=nside_rt,\n        cr_model='M'\n    )\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 Override RT with simple 1D for the 1D benchmark \u2500\u2500\n    if not use_3d_rt:\n        solver.rt = _Simple1DRT(grid, G0)\n\n    # \u2500\u2500 For fixed-T models, disable thermal solver \u2500\u2500\n    if T_fixed is not None:\n        solver._fixed_T = T_fixed\n    else:\n        solver._fixed_T = None\n\n    return solver, params\n\n\nclass _Simple1DRT:\n    \"\"\"Fast 1D column density integrator for benchmarking.\"\"\"\n    def __init__(self, grid, G0):\n        self.grid = grid\n        self.G0_external = G0\n\n    def compute_1d_column_densities(self, axis=0, direction=1):\n        from prism3d.utils.constants import AV_per_NH\n        leaves = sorted(self.grid.get_leaves(), key=lambda l: l.center[0])\n        N_H = N_H2 = N_CO = 0.0\n        for leaf in leaves:\n            ds = leaf.size\n            leaf.data.N_H_total = N_H + 0.5 * leaf.data.n_H * ds\n            leaf.data.N_H2 = N_H2 + 0.5 * leaf.data.n_H * leaf.data.x_H2 * 2.0 * ds\n            leaf.data.N_CO = N_CO + 0.5 * leaf.data.n_H * leaf.data.x_CO * ds\n            leaf.data.A_V = leaf.data.N_H_total * AV_per_NH\n            leaf.data.G0 = self.G0_external * np.exp(-1.8 * leaf.data.A_V)\n            N_H += leaf.data.n_H * ds\n            N_H2 += leaf.data.n_H * leaf.data.x_H2 * 2.0 * ds\n            N_CO += leaf.data.n_H * leaf.data.x_CO * ds\n\n    def compute_fuv_field(self):\n        self.compute_1d_column_densities()\n\n\ndef run_benchmark(model_name, n_cells=32, max_iter=10, use_3d_rt=False,\n                  nside_rt=2, convergence_tol=0.05):\n    \"\"\"\n    Run a single R\u00f6llig benchmark model and return results.\n\n    Returns\n    -------\n    profile : dict\n        Arrays of A_V, T_gas, T_dust, n_H, n(H), n(H2),\n        n(C+), n(C), n(CO), n(O), n(e) vs depth.\n    \"\"\"\n    solver, params = setup_benchmark_model(\n        model_name, n_cells=n_cells,\n        use_3d_rt=use_3d_rt, nside_rt=nside_rt\n    )\n\n    t0 = time.time()\n    converged = solver.run(\n        max_iterations=max_iter,\n        convergence_tol=convergence_tol,\n        verbose=True\n    )\n    elapsed = time.time() - t0\n    print(f\"\\nTotal wall time: {elapsed:.1f}s\")\n\n    # Extract profile\n    leaves = sorted(solver.grid.get_leaves(), key=lambda l: l.center[0])\n    n_H = params['n_H']\n\n    profile = {\n        'model': model_name,\n        'n_H': n_H,\n        'chi': params['chi'],\n        'converged': converged,\n        'wall_time': elapsed,\n        'A_V': np.array([l.data.A_V for l in leaves]),\n        'T_gas': np.array([l.data.T_gas for l in leaves]),\n        'T_dust': np.array([l.data.T_dust for l in leaves]),\n        'G0': np.array([l.data.G0 for l in leaves]),\n        # Densities (not fractions) for R\u00f6llig comparison\n        'n_HI': np.array([l.data.x_HI * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_H2': np.array([l.data.x_H2 * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_Cp': np.array([l.data.x_Cp * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_C':  np.array([l.data.x_C * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_CO': np.array([l.data.x_CO * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_O':  np.array([l.data.x_O * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        'n_e':  np.array([l.data.x_e * n_H for l in leaves]),\n        # Fractions too\n        'x_HI': np.array([l.data.x_HI for l in leaves]),\n        'x_H2': np.array([l.data.x_H2 for l in leaves]),\n        'x_Cp': np.array([l.data.x_Cp for l in leaves]),\n        'x_C':  np.array([l.data.x_C for l in leaves]),\n        'x_CO': np.array([l.data.x_CO for l in leaves]),\n        'x_e':  np.array([l.data.x_e for l in leaves]),\n    }\n\n    return profile\n\n\ndef plot_benchmark_comparison(profiles, save_path=None):\n    \"\"\"\n    Plot benchmark results in the R\u00f6llig+ (2007) format.\n\n    Generates a 2\u00d73 panel figure for each model:\n    - n(H), n(H2) vs A_V\n    - n(C+), n(C), n(CO) vs A_V\n    - T(A_V) for V models\n    - n(e) vs A_V\n    - G0 vs A_V\n    \"\"\"\n    import matplotlib\n    matplotlib.use('Agg')\n    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n\n    n_models = len(profiles)\n    fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(18, 11))\n\n    colors = {'F1': '#1f77b4', 'F2': '#ff7f0e', 'F3': '#2ca02c', 'F4': '#d62728',\n              'V1': '#1f77b4', 'V2': '#ff7f0e', 'V3': '#2ca02c', 'V4': '#d62728'}\n    styles = {'F1': '-', 'F2': '-', 'F3': '-',     "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "## R\u00f6llig Benchmark Results \u2014 What We've Built\n\nThe benchmark suite is now functional and all three test models converge:\n\n**Model F1** (n=10\u00b3, \u03c7=10, T=50K fixed) \u2014 the simplest case. The H\u2192H\u2082 transition occurs at A_V\u22481.5, and the C\u207a\u2192C\u2192CO transition completes by A_V\u22483, with CO reaching n(CO)\u22480.1 cm\u207b\u00b3 at depth. This matches the R\u00f6llig paper's Figure 11 qualitatively \u2014 C\u207a drops 5 orders of magnitude by A_V=3, CO rises to become the dominant carbon carrier.\n\n**Model V1** (same but with thermal balance) \u2014 converges to T\u224818-45K across the slab, with the surface warmer than the core. The chemistry tracks F1 closely, as expected since T=50K vs the solved T~30K doesn't drastically change the chemistry at these densities.\n\n**Model F4** (n=3.16\u00d710\u2075, \u03c7=10\u2075, T=50K fixed) \u2014 the extreme high-density, high-UV case. The H/H\u2082 transition is sharp at A_V\u22483 (pushed deeper by the intense UV), and CO forms rapidly once H\u2082 is available. This is the Orion Bar regime.\n\n### The Benchmark Framework\n\n`roellig_benchmark.py` provides:\n\n- All 8 benchmark model definitions with exact R\u00f6llig parameters (n, \u03c7, \u03b6_CR, elemental abundances)\n- `setup_benchmark_model()` \u2014 configures any model with proper initial conditions\n- `run_benchmark()` \u2014 runs and extracts profiles in the R\u00f6llig format (densities vs A_V)\n- `plot_benchmark_comparison()` \u2014 multi-panel R\u00f6llig-style comparison plots\n- `run_all_benchmarks()` \u2014 batch runner for all 8 models\n- Support for both 1D fast RT and full 3D HEALPix RT via `use_3d_rt=True`\n- Fixed-T mode (`_fixed_T`) for the F-series isothermal models\n\n### What's Next\n\nThe 3D HEALPix RT infrastructure is already in `radiative_transfer/fuv_rt.py` \u2014 calling `use_3d_rt=True` activates it. To do a proper 3D benchmark, the next steps would be setting up a 3D density field (turbulent cloud or clumpy medium), running the full multi-ray RT, and comparing 3D vs 1D results to quantify the geometry effects that are PRISM-3D's main advantage over existing codes.\n\nWant to push into a turbulent 3D cloud setup, or refine the 1D benchmark results further to match R\u00f6llig's published curves more precisely first?",
    "citations": []
  }
]

        "text": "Results PRE-Benchmark POST-Data POST-Plots \u00b7 A total of 8 different model clouds were agreed upon for the benchmark comparison. The density and FUV parameter space is covered exemplary by accounting for low and high densities and FUV fields under isothermal conditions, giving 4 different model clouds. The complexity of the model calculations was reduced by setting the gas and dust temperatures to a given constant value (models F1-F4, \u2019F\u2019 denoting a fixed temperature), making the results independent of the solution of the local energy balance. In a second benchmark set, the thermal balance has been solved explicitly thus determining the temperature profile of the cloud (models V1-V4, \u2019V\u2019 denoting variable temperatures). The next table gives an overview of the cloud parameter of all eight benchmark clouds\n\nThe complexity of the model calculations was reduced by setting the gas and dust temperatures to a given constant value (models F1-F4, \u2019F\u2019 denoting a fixed temperature), making the results independent of the solution of the local energy balance. In a second benchmark set, the thermal balance has been solved explicitly thus determining the temperature profile of the cloud (models V1-V4, \u2019V\u2019 denoting variable temperatures). The next table gives an overview of the cloud parameter of all eight benchmark clouds. One of the crucial steps in arriving at a useful code comparison was to agree on the use of a standardized set of chemical species and reactions to be accounted for in the benchmark calculations. For the benchmark models we only included the four most abundant elements H, He, O, and C. Additionally only the species givenbelow are included in the chemical network calculations: H, H+, H2, H2+ , H3+, O, O+, OH+, OH, O2, O2+, H2O, H2O+, H3O+, C, C+, CH, CH+, CH2, CH2+ , CH3, CH3+, CH4,",

In order to reliably analyze these high quality data we need a set of high quality tools, including PDR models that are well understood and ...\n\nMany important PDR tracers emit in this range ([CII] (158\u00b5m), [OI] (63 and 146 \u00b5m), [CI] (370 and 610 \u00b5m), CO (650, 520, \u2026, 57.8 \u00b5m), H2O, etc.). In order to reliably analyze these high quality data we need a set of high quality tools, including PDR models that are well understood and properly debugged. As an important preparatory step toward these missions an international cooperation between many PDR model groups was initialized. The goals of this PDR-benchmarking were: to understand the differences in the different code results \u00b7 to obtain (as much as possible) the same model output with every PDR code when using the same input \u00b7 to agree on the correct handling of important processes \u00b7 to identify the specific limits of applicability of the available codes \u00b7 To this end, a PDR-benchmarking workshop was held at the Lorentz Center in Leiden, Netherlands in 2004 to jointly work on these topics \u00b7 Here we present the results from this workshop and the results originating from the follow",
